dbVersion = 40
enrolledProgram = none
activePath = none
workoutSessions = 12
Then advance time to cross a gap threshold (≥4d quiet stretch, ≥14d welcome-back).

Three macrocycles that walk a true beginner from movement literacy to self-programming: learn the patterns and effort (Foundations) → build real strength under load (Build) → peak, periodize, and take the wheel (Own It).

Three macrocycles that walk a true beginner from movement literacy to self-programming: learn the patterns and effort (Foundations) → build real strength under load (Build) → peak, periodize, and take the wheel (Own It).

Two macrocycles that build on each other: grow by doing more work (Accumulation — Volume), then add heavier, tension-focused training (Tension — Mechanical). Each is its own arc of blocks ending in a deload, with a light active-rest week between them. ~25 weeks total.

Two macrocycles that build on each other: add the muscle that produces force (Base — Hypertrophy), then turn it into strength (Build — Strength). Each is its own arc of blocks ending in a deload, with a light active-rest week between them. ~25 weeks total.

A 2-macrocycle movement plan. Consistency is the goal, not load. The second macrocycle offers an optional switch to a different set of movements — a new way to move the body — at the same easy effort.

Three macrocycles that walk a true beginner from movement literacy to self-programming: learn the patterns and effort (Foundations) → build real strength under load (Build) → peak, periodize, and take the wheel (Own It).
A Path connects your training to what actually matters — evidence-backed goals for mental health, bone strength, brain function, and more.
Choose what matters to you. Select up to 2 paths.

Resistance training builds brain, not just muscle.
Load your bones to keep them.
e.g. knee surgery, COVID, a broken wrist

We'll pre-load the most common equipment for you.
Equipment you own or share a space.
Chains, full rack rooms, cardio floors.
An overhead press that rotates the palms from facing-in at the bottom to facing-out at the top, recruiting all three heads of the deltoid.
Extend a band overhead from behind the head to full lockout, targeting the long head of the triceps through a stretched position.
Anchor a band overhead and push it down to full elbow extension, isolating the triceps with constant band tension.
Suspended on parallel bars, lower the body by bending the elbows and press back up; leaning forward biases the chest, staying upright the triceps.
Lying on a bench, lower a barbell to the mid-chest and press it back up to full lockout, keeping the shoulder blades retracted and feet planted.
Press a barbell from the front of the shoulders to a full overhead lockout while keeping the core braced and ribs down.
Lying on a bench, lower a barbell toward the forehead and extend back up, loading the triceps more heavily than the dumbbell version.
Pull a barbell up the front of the body to chest height with high elbows, working the side delts and traps.
Whip two heavy ropes in alternating waves for time, a shoulder-and-grip-driven conditioning effort that keeps the trunk braced throughout.
With the hands on a bench behind you, lower the hips by bending the elbows and press back up to train the triceps with bodyweight.
Bring two cable handles together in front of the body in an arc, keeping constant tension on the chest throughout the range.
Raise a low cable out to the side to shoulder height, keeping constant tension on the side delt through the whole range.
Bench press with a shoulder-width grip and tucked elbows, shifting the load onto the triceps while still training the chest.
Press a barbell on a declined bench, biasing the lower portion of the chest through a shortened, lower-shoulder-stress range.
For a new movement, we'll start near your weight on the lift you pick, then adjust from your first set.
